does: This paper introduces a unified defense framework that jointly analyzes both user prompts and LLM-generated responses to detect harmful interactions before deployment. The core contribution is moving beyond isolated analysis to examine the relationship between adversarial intent in prompts and harmful content in outputs, using specialized modules for intent assessment and harm detection paired with a conflict-resolution Judge component. The framework formalizes a threat model for prompt-response attacks and evaluates its effectiveness across five attack categories: jailbreaks, prompt injection, phishing, cyber abuse, and harmful content, demonstrating superior detection compared to prompt-only or response-only approaches.
connects: This work extends prior defenses against adversarial inputs to LLMs, building on safety verification literature that has typically isolated analysis to either prompts or outputs. The research responds to the recognized limitations of existing mitigation strategies that fail to capture attacks where benign-looking prompts produce harmful outputs, or vice versa. It draws on threat modeling frameworks from security research and combines concepts from prompt injection defense and content moderation literature. The multi-category evaluation approach connects to benchmark-driven safety research in large language models, while the formalization of threat models relates to broader AI safety verification methodologies that treat alignment as a formal verification problem.
enables: This framework establishes infrastructure for real-time safety verification in production LLM deployments, opening pathways for more sophisticated adversarial robustness testing. By demonstrating joint prompt-response analysis, the work enables development of deeper defenses that understand attack semantics rather than surface patterns. Future research can extend the Judge module to handle more nuanced conflict resolution, scale the framework to more threat categories, and integrate it with adaptive defenses that modify model behavior mid-interaction. The formalized threat model provides a foundation for both red-teaming and for developing provably safer LLM systems, potentially informing constitutional AI and interpretability research aimed at understanding how intent propagates through model computations to outputs.
